LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT KEENWAWA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO THE FIRST, SECOND AND SIXTH CAUSES OF ACTION IN THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT FILED BY PLAINTIFF CLAUDIA RUBIO IS OVERRULED AS TO THE FIRST AND SECOND CAUSES OF ACTION FOR VIOLATION OF THE FEHA AND SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE SIXTH CAUSE OF ACTION FOR VIOLATION OF LABOR CODE 226(A).(SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS) ORDER IS SIGNED. MASTER CALENDAR COURT

Off Calendar Jan-15-2019 Continued To Mar-06-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-20-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Arlo Uriarte, Esq., Liberation Law Group, P.C, Telephone 415-695-1000, appearing for Plaintiff;
Gal Gressel, Esq., Littler Mendelson, P.C., Telephone 415-677-3175, appearing for Defendant Keenwawa dba Eatsa;

RULING - ARGUED: The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Keenwawa, Inc.'s demurrer to the first, second and sixth causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Claudia Rubio is overruled as to the first and second causes of action for violation of the FEHA and sustained without leave to amend as to the sixth cause of action for violation of Labor Code 226(a). While no reported case has been cited that permits verification of a DFEH administrative complaint by someone other than the claimant or her counsel, no reported case has been cited that holds that the only persons who can verify a DFEH administrative complaint are the claimant and her counsel. As Keenwawa acknowledges, the DFEH regulations permit a non-attorney authorized representative to verify an administrative complaint and broad language regarding the liberal construction and remedial nature of the FEHA in Blum v. Superior Court (2006) 14 Cal. App. 4th 418 reasonably supports allowing an authorized non-attorney (at least one working for the claimant's counsel) to verify a DFEH administrative complaint. Nothing in the first amended complaint or the judicially noticeable facts shows that Ms. Rubio did not authorize Mr. Coria to verify her administrative complaint. Nor has Keenwawa identified any persuasive reason grounded in the policies underlying the FEHA why it should be immune from FEHA liability due to the fortuity that Mr. Coria rather than an attorney in his firm verified the administrative complaint. Labor Code 226(a) does not require that a wage statement state the number of sick days an employee is entitled to receive. The language and logic of Maldonado v. Epsilon Plastics, Inc. (2018) 22 Cal. App. 5th 1308 precludes a claim for violation of Labor Code 226(a) based on the allegation that a wage statement lacks information about the amount of rest break compensation an employee claims but did not receive. Because Ms. Rubio has not identified any way she can state a viable claim for violation of Labor Code 226(a), she is not given leave to amend her sixth cause of action. Order is signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)
